_meta.verificationNote: Risk treatment strategies constructed with NIST CSF 2.0-aligned examples. Treatment options follow ISO 31000 and NIST SP 800-30 guidance. Examples are indicative for organizational adaptation.

title: Cyber Risk Treatment Options
description: Four standard risk treatment strategies with CSF 2.0-aligned examples, selection criteria, and implementation considerations. Organizations should apply the most appropriate strategy per risk based on cost-benefit analysis, risk appetite, and operational context.

strategies.name: Mitigate (Reduce)
strategies.description: Implement additional controls or enhance existing controls to reduce the likelihood, impact, or both of the identified risk to an acceptable level within the organization's risk appetite.
whenToUse: Residual risk exceeds organizational risk appetite
Cost-effective controls are available to reduce risk
Risk cannot be avoided or transferred due to operational necessity
Regulatory requirements mandate specific control implementations
considerations: Prioritize controls that address root cause over symptoms
Consider defense-in-depth — layered controls across prevention, detection, and response
Validate that new controls do not introduce unacceptable operational friction
Establish metrics to measure control effectiveness post-implementation

csfAlignedExamples.example: Define and communicate formal risk appetite thresholds to ensure consistent risk acceptance decisions across the organization
Deploy automated asset discovery and CMDB integration to reduce unmanaged asset exposure
Implement zero-trust architecture with continuous authentication and microsegmentation to reduce unauthorized access risk
Expand SIEM log source coverage and deploy behavioral analytics to reduce detection blind spots
Develop and regularly test incident response playbooks for high-probability attack scenarios to reduce response time
Implement automated failover and immutable backups to reduce recovery time and data loss potential

strategies.name: Transfer (Share)
strategies.description: Shift the financial impact or operational burden of the risk to a third party through insurance, outsourcing, or contractual arrangements, while retaining accountability for managing the risk.
whenToUse: Financial impact is the primary concern and is insurable
Specialized expertise is required that the organization cannot maintain internally
Contractual mechanisms can effectively allocate risk to parties better positioned to manage it
Residual risk after mitigation still exceeds appetite for financial impact dimension
considerations: Transfer does not eliminate reputational or regulatory accountability
Cyber insurance policies have coverage limits, exclusions, and conditions — review carefully
Outsourced services require ongoing vendor risk management
Transfer arrangements must be documented and reviewed annually

csfAlignedExamples.example: Include cybersecurity liability, breach notification, and indemnification clauses in vendor contracts to transfer supply chain risk
Purchase cyber insurance to transfer residual financial risk from data breach scenarios after mitigation controls are in place
Engage managed detection and response (MDR) provider to transfer operational burden of 24/7 threat monitoring and analysis
Establish forensic investigation retainer with specialized firm to transfer forensic capability requirements

strategies.name: Accept (Retain)
strategies.description: Consciously decide to retain the risk without additional treatment because the residual risk level falls within the organization's defined risk appetite, or the cost of treatment exceeds the expected loss.
whenToUse: Residual risk is within defined organizational risk appetite thresholds
Cost of further mitigation exceeds expected loss from risk realization
Risk is inherently low likelihood and impact
Temporary acceptance pending planned mitigation in a future budget cycle
considerations: Acceptance must be a formal, documented decision — not a default from inaction
Risk acceptance authority must match the risk level (higher risk requires more senior approval)
Accepted risks must remain on the risk register and be reviewed on schedule
Set trigger conditions that would require re-evaluation of the acceptance decision
Temporary acceptance must include a timeline for treatment implementation

csfAlignedExamples.example: Accept residual risk of operating a legacy system for 12 months while replacement project is completed, with compensating controls documented
Accept the low residual risk of a non-critical informational website with no sensitive data after basic security controls are applied
Accept the risk of unencrypted non-sensitive public data after confirming data classification excludes any PII or proprietary content

strategies.name: Avoid (Eliminate)
strategies.description: Eliminate the risk entirely by discontinuing the activity, system, or process that creates the risk. This removes the threat-vulnerability pair from the environment.
whenToUse: Risk cannot be reduced to an acceptable level through mitigation or transfer
Cost of controls exceeds the value of the activity or asset being protected
Alternative approaches exist that achieve the same business outcome without the risk
Regulatory or safety implications make the risk categorically unacceptable
considerations: Avoidance may limit business capability or opportunity — assess trade-offs explicitly
Verify that the alternative approach does not introduce equivalent or greater risks
Document the business justification for discontinuing the activity
Communicate the decision and its implications to affected stakeholders

csfAlignedExamples.example: Decommission end-of-life systems that cannot be patched or adequately secured, eliminating the vulnerability entirely
Discontinue collection of unnecessary personal data to eliminate data breach risk for that data category
Terminate vendor relationship where the vendor cannot demonstrate adequate security controls and no suitable compensating controls exist
Migrate from self-managed infrastructure to managed cloud services to avoid risks associated with maintaining security configurations for complex on-premises environments
